attributes.description: Short summary of the activity, shown in lists and timelines.
attributes.name: subject
attributes.usage: The headline. Drives list-view legibility and search. Should briefly describe what happened or what needs to happen ("Discussed Q2 renewal," "Send proposal," "Birthday card").

attributes.description: Discriminator that distinguishes the kind of interaction.
attributes.name: activityType
attributes.usage: In some systems this is implicit because activity types are separate entities (Salesforce Task vs Event; EspoCRM Task vs Meeting vs Call). In CiviCRM and Bloomerang, this is an explicit enum/lookup. Drives the iconography, available fields, and reporting categorization. Often heavily customized in nonprofit deployments to capture mission-specific activity types (Site Visit, Mentoring Session, Workshop Attendance).

attributes.name: activityStatus
attributes.usage: Distinguishes work-to-do (Not Started, In Progress) from work-done (Completed) from work-abandoned (Canceled, Deferred). Drives "open tasks" filtering, dashboards, and overdue alerts. Salesforce uses Status on Tasks; Events have no equivalent because events are scheduled rather than statused. Bloomerang's Interactions are typically log entries (already-happened) so status is less central.

attributes.description: Date the activity occurred (for past activities) or is scheduled (for future).
attributes.name: activityDate
attributes.usage: Anchors timeline ordering. For Tasks this is the due date. For Calls and Meetings this is the date the interaction happened or is scheduled. For Emails this is the date sent or received.

attributes.description: Date by which the task should be completed (task-specific).
attributes.name: dueDate
attributes.usage: Drives overdue alerts, sorting on task lists, and date-based automation. Distinct from activityDate when an activity is scheduled for one date but due (results required) by another. Typically only populated on Task-type activities.

attributes.description: Start date and time of the activity (for time-bound events).
attributes.name: dateStart
attributes.usage: Calendar-event coordinate. Required for Meeting and Call types in most systems. Combined with dateEnd determines duration. Drives calendar UI, conflict-detection, and meeting-reminder automation.

attributes.description: End date and time of the activity.
attributes.name: dateEnd
attributes.usage: Combined with dateStart determines duration. Required for events that span time (meetings, calls). Drives capacity-planning reports ("hours spent in meetings this week"). Some systems compute duration automatically from start + duration rather than storing dateEnd.

attributes.description: Free-form narrative of what happened or what needs to happen.
attributes.name: description
attributes.usage: Where the actual content of the interaction lives — call notes, meeting outcomes, email body summaries, task instructions. Often the most-read field on an Activity record because it captures the substance the rest of the structured fields don't. In Bloomerang Interactions this is the primary content field.

attributes.description: Polymorphic reference to the primary record this activity is logged against (Account, Contact, Opportunity, Case, etc.).
attributes.name: parent
attributes.notes: Polymorphism support varies. Salesforce, EspoCRM, CiviCRM model polymorphic activity-to-anything cleanly. HubSpot Engagements associate via Associations API. Bloomerang Interactions associate with Constituents only (no Account vs Contact distinction).
attributes.usage: Anchors the activity to its primary subject record so it appears on that record's activity timeline. Polymorphic — can point at any of several entity types. Salesforce uses WhatId (Account/Opportunity/ Case/etc) and WhoId (Contact/Lead) as separate fields. EspoCRM uses a single linkParent field with entityList = [Account, Contact, Lead, Opportunity, Case]. HubSpot uses Associations. CiviCRM uses ActivityContact junction records.

attributes.description: The user the activity is assigned to (the person responsible).
attributes.name: assignedUser
attributes.usage: For Tasks: who needs to do this work. For Meetings: who organized. For Calls: who placed/received. Drives "my tasks" filtering, workload reports, and reassignment workflows. Almost universal — every activity needs an accountable user.

attributes.name: priority
attributes.usage: Drives sort order in task lists and dashboards. Most useful for Task-type activities where some are urgent and others routine. Typically not populated on Meetings, Calls, or completed Emails.

attributes.description: System-generated timestamp of when the activity was created.
attributes.name: createdAt
attributes.usage: Audit field. Distinct from activityDate (when the activity is/was scheduled). Drives "tasks created this week" reports and integration sync filters.

business_context: Activity records are how CRMs track work-done and interactions-had. They serve three distinct purposes that often get confused: (1) communication log — record of outbound/inbound contact (call notes, email summaries, meeting outcomes); (2) task tracking — assigned to-do items with due dates and owners; (3) calendar — scheduled future events with start/end times and attendees. Different CRMs split these three differently: Salesforce uses Task (open/closed work) and Event (scheduled meeting) with shared "Activity" reporting. HubSpot lumps everything under "Engagements" with subtypes (CALL, EMAIL, MEETING, TASK, NOTE, COMMUNICATION). Attio treats Tasks as the core activity primitive and has separate Meetings, Calls, and Emails. EspoCRM models Task, Meeting, Call as distinct entities. CiviCRM unifies under Activity with an activity_type_id discriminator. Bloomerang uses Interactions (a unified history-log entity). Nonprofit deployments often add specialized activity types: site visit, mentoring session, volunteer shift, donation acknowledgment letter sent. When interviewing clients, surface "Activity" by asking how they want to track their interactions: do they need to assign and track tasks (with due dates and assignees), schedule future meetings on a shared calendar, log past phone calls and emails as history, or all three? Each requires different CRM features and may map to different entities depending on backend.
name: Activity
purpose: A unit of interaction or work logged against another record (typically an Account, Contact, Opportunity, or Case). Includes phone calls, meetings, tasks, emails sent or received, and other touchpoints. The base type from which most communication and task entities derive in CRMs.

relationships.description: An activity is typically logged against an account.
An activity is typically logged against a contact (or several).
An activity is assigned to a user.

sources.title: Bloomerang REST API documentation: Interaction endpoint

systems.name: Tasks (with separate Meetings, Calls, Emails)
systems.notes: Attio has a first-class Tasks entity for work items, plus Meetings (auto-created from calendar sync), Calls (call recordings), and Emails (synced from Gmail/Outlook). Communication intelligence attributes on Person and Company surface aggregate stats (last interaction, first interaction, next due task) without requiring direct activity queries.

systems.name: Interaction
systems.notes: Single unified Interaction entity for tracking all communication and engagement history with constituents. Interaction types include Email, Phone Call, In-Person, Letter, Text Message, Social Media, Volunteer Hours. Notes are a separate entity in Bloomerang, not an Interaction subtype. Tasks are not a first-class entity in Bloomerang — task management is typically handled outside the CRM.

systems.name: Activity
systems.notes: Single unified Activity entity with activity_type_id discriminator (Meeting, Phone Call, Email, SMS, Print/Mail, Pledge Acknowledgment, Membership Renewal Reminder, Survey, Petition Signature, etc.). Activity types are extensible — admins can add custom types. Activities are linked to Contacts via ActivityContact records (target, assignee, source) supporting many-to-many.

systems.name: Task, Meeting, Call
systems.notes: EspoCRM models Task (work item with due date), Meeting (calendar event with attendees), and Call (phone call event) as three separate first-class entities. All three use a polymorphic `parent` field (linkParent type) that can point at Account, Contact, Lead, Opportunity, or Case.

systems.api_name: engagements (subtypes: CALL, EMAIL, MEETING, TASK, NOTE, COMMUNICATION)
systems.name: Engagement
systems.notes: Engagements is the unified API; subtypes distinguish Call, Email, Meeting, Task, Note, Communication (SMS / WhatsApp / LinkedIn). Note is included as an engagement type, which makes Notes activity-like in HubSpot's data model.

systems.name: Activity (Task + Event)
systems.notes: MAJOR DIVERGENCE. Salesforce splits Activity into Task (open or closed work item with due date) and Event (scheduled appointment with start/end times). They share a virtual "Activity" reporting layer but are physically separate objects with different fields. Email and Call are typically modeled as Tasks with TaskSubtype set.

systems.name: Task, Event (with NPSP-specific activity types)
systems.notes: Same as Salesforce. NPSP commonly adds custom Task subtypes for donor stewardship: Thank You Letter Sent, Acknowledgment Pending, Major Gift Cultivation, Stewardship Call. Tasks linked to Opportunities (donations) drive donor-acknowledgment workflows.
